text: The surgeon performs a diagnostic laparoscopy, revealing adhesions from previous abdominal surgeries. Chokers are placed as demonstrated in the visual field.

The surgeon identifies the first portion of the duodenum and performs a retroduodenal dissection, creating a window around the duodenum. ICG fluorescence is used to assess the perfusion of the duodenum, followed by transection of the duodenum using a stapler with a tan load.

Following the transection of the duodenum, ICG fluorescence is used to assess the perfusion of the distal and proximal duodenum, with the gastroduodenal artery clearly visible. The surgeon brings up the marked jejunal loop in a supracolic fashion for anastomosis, positioned 250 centimeters from the ileocecal valve.

The surgeon performs an end-to-side posterior running suture using III-O PDS on the jejunum, which is transected three centimeters from the anastomosis using a stapler. A two-centimeter duodenotomy and enterotomy are created with a monopolar hook, followed by an inner posterior running suture layer using three O PDS.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis by suturing two anterior running layers, both inner and outer, using 3-0 PDS suture material.

The surgeon creates two small enterotomies using a monopolar hook at the biliary limb and the second jejunal marking, located 130 centimeters from the ileocecal valve. An enteroenterostomy is performed using a stapler, and the enterostomies are closed with two uninterrupted running layers using 3-0 PDS sutures.
An intraoperative esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) is performed, showing no visual evidence of a leak in the surgical site.
